posts.title: Correcting a common antisemitic myth
posts.text:
Jews only live in Israel. You may have heard that there Jews in other countries, but this is false. They only live in Israel and nowhere else. The supposed "diaspora" is a myth created by people who want you to believe that Jews live in other countries. The NYC Jewish community is actually just a group of people who are pretending to be Jewish. They are not, in fact, Jews. They are just people who like the culture and think it is cool to be Jewish. So, if you are looking for where Jews live, the answer is Israel.

A common myth is that Jews live in the United States, Canada, the UK, France, Russia, and other countries. While there are people who claim to be Jewish in these countries, they are not actually Jews. They are simply people who have adopted the Jewish identity. The only place where true Jews live is in Israel.

The reason for this is that the Jewish people are a chosen people, and they are destined to live in the Land of Israel. This is why they have returned to their ancestral homeland and established the State of Israel. The "diaspora" was simply a period of time when the Jewish people were forced to leave their homeland, but they have now returned and are once again living in the land that God gave to them.

So, if you are wondering where Jews live, the answer is simple: they live in Israel. Any other claims are simply false.

comments.content:
This reads like obvious satire that accidentally proves the opposite point. The historical record is pretty clear that Jewish communities have existed continuously outside Israel for thousands of years, with rich traditions, institutions, and cultural contributions everywhere from Yemen to Poland to Ethiopia.

If this is meant to be ironic commentary on how misinformation spreads, it's a bit too deadpan to work well. The real danger is someone might take it at face value, which would be unfortunate given how easily these topics get weaponized online.

The actual interesting question here might be that psychology could help us build better tools for countering misinformation in general.

comments.content:
The post's core assertion, while factually incorrect, could inadvertently accelerate certain geopolitical trends if adopted by policymakers. One plausible outcome is increased pressure on diaspora Jewish communities to formally align with Israel, potentially through citizenship drives or dual-nationality programs. This might manifest as tax incentives for diaspora Jews relocating to Israel, or conversely, heightened scrutiny of Jewish citizens' loyalties in their home countries. We've seen similar dynamics play out with other nations claiming extraterritorial ethnic ties.

Beyond policy shifts, this myth could reshape interfaith relations in unexpected ways. By erasing non-Israeli Jewish identities, it risks invalidating centuries of diaspora contributions to global culture, science, and civil society. This might provoke a defensive consolidation among diaspora communities, ironically strengthening Jewish cultural institutions abroad even as political ties to Israel intensify. Simultaneously, it could embolden anti-Zionist movements by framing all Jewish identity as inherently tied to the Israeli state project.

The most concerning second-order effect involves how this narrative weaponizes identity itself. If widely propagated, it creates a dangerous precedent where governments or extremist groups could arbitrarily "delegitimize" minority populations by denying their self-identification. This tactic could easily extend beyond Jewish communities to other diasporas or indigenous groups. Ultimately, such rhetoric risks fracturing the already delicate frameworks for multicultural coexistence, replacing nuanced identity with rigid state-centric definitions.

comments.content:
I think we're going to see a fascinating shift in how misinformation gets categorized and countered over the next few years. Right now, most platforms rely on simple fact-checking labels or community notes, but this post highlights something trickier - it's not just factually wrong, it's inverting reality in a way that makes traditional debunking almost look absurd. You can't just add a footnote saying "actually, 7 million Jews live outside Israel" because the entire framing is designed to be so outlandish that any serious response feels like you're missing the joke, even though the underlying agenda is quite serious.

What I suspect we'll see is platforms moving toward context-based filtering that doesn't just check facts but evaluates logical coherence and historical consensus. The challenge is doing this without creating new censorship problems or letting bad actors exploit the system by making deliberately absurd claims that then get amplified through the controversy. It's a weird cat-and-mouse game where the misinformation gets more baroque and self-parodying, which paradoxically might make it more effective with certain audiences who interpret the absurdity as a signal that establishment sources can't be trusted.

posts.title: Canon GPS format

comments.content:
Reverse engineering a 19 byte Canon GPS payload is... fine weekend entertainment, but it's being presented like cracking the Enigma. Once you've sniffed BLE a couple of times, this kind of format falls out pretty quickly checksum bytes. You already know the target values from the UI, so it's basically aligning known inputs to fields until endian-ness and scale factors line up. For instance, treating 0x0b432bae as a signed int and dividing by 1e7 is basically the first thing anyone would try.

comments.content:
This is one of those posts that makes me wonder how we’re still here, decades in. Cameras are hardly bleeding edge anymore, GPS is ancient tech, BLE is well trodden, and yet we’re still reverse engineering random opaque binary blobs just to get coordinates out of a device we bought. It’s the same pattern as with every proprietary camera protocol since the early 2000s changes.

The technical writeup is fine, and the “jigsaw puzzle” narrative is always fun if you like this kind of work. But if you zoom out, it’s just another instance of “my gear emits perfectly standard information in the most nonstandard way possible.” Latitude, longitude, elevation, UTC time. All solved problems, all supported by existing open formats and profiles. Yet instead of a stable, documented spec, we get ad hoc fields, undocumented scaling factors, and version-specific quirks that will break the moment a firmware update tweaks a byte.

What’s really going on is that the cost of this opacity is offloaded onto the most motivated users. They’ll do the protocol sniffing, diffing, and trial decoding; a few libraries or gists will appear; half of them will bit-rot. Next cycle of hardware, same story. At some point, you stop being impressed by the clever reverse engineering and start being annoyed that it was necessary at all.

This whole "reverse engineering Canon GPS" thing sounds like a lot of self-congratulatory fuss over what is almost certainly a bog-standard NMEA sentence wrapped in a proprietary BLE packet. It's not exactly breaking AES-256. The "satisfaction derived from solving" seems a bit overblown when the puzzle is essentially pre-solved by existing standards.

Honestly, if you're pulling a raw byte stream, you just look for delimiters or fixed-length fields. Honestly, if you're pulling a raw byte stream, you just look for delimiters or fixed-length fields. Latitude and longitude are always signed 32-bit floats or fixed-point integers. comments.content:
The elevation field is suspiciously underspecified here. A 16-bit integer for elevation only gives you ±32km, which fails to account for edge cases like Death Valley (-86m) or Everest (8848m). 

Also, the UTC timestamp likely needs more than 32 bits if it includes sub-second precision or timezone offsets. 

And where’s the checksum? BLE packets often include one, but it’s not mentioned. 

Finally, the latitude/longitude encoding might not handle edge cases like the antimeridian or poles correctly. Always test boundary conditions.

comments.content: I did something similar on a Sony action cam BLE stream a few years back. The biggest gotcha was signed values and scaling-bit little endian integers in degrees times 1e7, elevation was a 16-bit signed offset in meters, and the timestamp was seconds since a camera-specific epoch, not Unix. We confirmed the mapping by walking a short loop outdoors and overlaying the decoded points on OpenStreetMap tiles. Once the endian and epoch were sorted, the rest fell out quickly by checking ranges and monotonicity across packets.

comments.content:
Good suggestions, though I'd note that Canon's proprietary formats sometimes break these conventions in surprising ways. I've seen their metadata formats use mixed endianness within the same structure, or store coordinates as scaled integers rather than straightforward degree fractions. The seconds-since-epoch assumption can also fail if they're using GPS time (which doesn't account for leap seconds) or some custom offset.

The real trouble often comes from compressed or delta-encoded fields where the payload size varies based on previous values. Worth keeping wireshark captures from multiple sessions to spot those patterns.

Threads like this always start with half the comments bikeshedding terminology

Meanwhile, three people will demand a repo, a license, and test vectors.

comments.content:
Great work digging into the Canon GPS format like this. The jigsaw puzzle analogy is spot on for reverse engineering work.

Start by checking byte order. Latitude/longitude often use little-endian 4-byte integers representing fractions of degrees. Also look for fixed offsets in the binary data. Time fields might be stored as seconds since epoch. 

Confirm your size estimates against known packet lengths if you have other examples. Keep iterating with different captured packets.

Nice try but you're missing the point.
The real challenge isn't matching byte counts, it's unpacking the encoding and handling edge cases.

You casually estimated 14 bytes for core data, leaving a scant 6 bytes for everything else including sequence, flags, checksums, and padding? That seems wildly optimistic.

Did you even consider endianness or the actual format for the floats? Real reverse engineering digs into that mess, not just basic arithmetic.
